\Section{Validation via Specification Inference}
\label{sec:validation}

\begin{WideTable}
    \caption{Inference test categories that exercise behavioral predicates.}
    \label{tab:inference-tests}
    \centering
    \small
    \begin{tabularx}{\linewidth}{l X}
      \hline
      \textbf{Category} & \textbf{Representative cases} \\
      \hline
      Direct calls &
        Single and chained calls to a spec'd helper; inferred specs use
        \texttt{ensures\_of} and \texttt{aborts\_of} to abstract over the
        callee's effect rather than inlining its body. \\
      \hline
      Mutual recursion &
        Mutually recursive \texttt{is\_even}/\texttt{is\_odd}; inference
        breaks the cycle by phrasing each spec in terms of the other's
        behavioral predicate. \\
      \hline
      Higher-order parameters &
        A function that takes a closure and applies it; the inferred spec
        abstracts the closure via \texttt{ensures\_of}/\texttt{aborts\_of}
        on the parameter, with no commitment to a particular implementation. \\
      \hline
      Stored-closure dispatch (enum) &
        Calculator with an enum variant carrying a closure field;
        dispatch through the variant is summarised by behavioral
        predicates on that field. \\
      \hline
      Stored-closure dispatch (struct field) &
        Vault with a \texttt{Strategy} closure stored in a struct field on
        Aptos framework types; inference emits \texttt{modifies\_of} for
        the field together with the field-form behavioral predicates. \\
      \hline
      Pre/post state labels &
        \texttt{move\_to}, \texttt{move\_from}, mutable resource indexing;
        inferred specs distinguish \texttt{@pre} from post-state on the
        memory the predicate observes. \\
      \hline
      Intermediate state labels &
        Sequenced state-modifying calls (e.g.\ remove-then-publish);
        inferred specs introduce existentially quantified intermediate
        labels and pin them to defining behavioral predicates. \\
      \hline
    \end{tabularx}
\end{WideTable}

Together with the introduction of behavioral predicates and function values, specification inference based on \emph{weakest precondition analysis} \cite{dijkstra1975wp} was added to \MVP.
One can think of \WP computation as an inversion of verification: given arbitrary code, specifications are generated which are then supposed to pass verification, and precisely describe the code.
This enables a validation pipeline where we go code $\leadsto$ code+specs $\leadsto$ VC, with verification expected to succeed.
This pipeline is tremendously helpful in AI-supported development, as it gives a baseline to AI to validate correctness of generated or provided code.
Tab.~\ref{tab:inference-tests} gives an overview of the current behavioral-predicate-related tests using this pipeline --- including function value parameters and struct fields.

\WP uses behavioral predicates to specify the effect of calls to other functions.
This enables formulating the \WP of one function in a modular fashion, abstracting from the called function.
Here is a simple example:

% @formatter:off
\begin{MoveBox}
  use account::other;
  fun incr(x: u64): u64 {
    other::func(x) + 1
  }
  spec incr {
    pragma opaque;
    ensures [inferred]
      result == result_of<other::func>(x)
  }
\end{MoveBox}
% @formatter:on

\noindent Without behavioral predicates, \WP analysis results would have been impractical to represent in Move source code, as we could only inline specs, leading to an explosion of condition sizes.


\noindent
